chunk_summary: Complementary feeding should start at six months, focusing on developmental readiness and iron-rich first foods to prevent growth faltering and iron deficiency.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs of developmental readiness for complementary feeding.
Learn appropriate first foods and their preparation for infants starting solids.
Recognize the risks of early or delayed introduction of complementary feeding.
Identify foods to avoid during the first year, including allergenic and unsafe items.
key_facts: Complementary feeding begins after six months (around 180 days) while continuing breastfeeding.
Developmental readiness includes sitting with support, steady head control, reduced tongue-thrust reflex, and interest in family food.
Delaying complementary feeding beyond seven months increases risks of growth faltering and iron deficiency.
First foods should be thick, energy-dense, and iron-rich, such as rice porridge, mashed khichdi, or ragi porridge.
Avoid honey in the first year due to botulism risk; allergenic foods like egg and peanut can be introduced in age-safe forms.
Watery gruels should be avoided as they lack adequate calories; enrich foods with ghee or oil for energy density.
reference_quote: Start complementary feeding after completion of 6 months (around 180 days) while continuing breastfeeding. Readiness is assessed clinically: the infant can sit with support and hold the head steady, shows interest in family food, and the tongue-thrust reflex is reduced so food is not automatically pushed out. (WHO, Guiding Principles for Complementary Feeding of the Breastfed Child)
document_key_topics: Risks of early or delayed complementary feeding
Food safety and allergenic food introduction
